Université catholique de Louvain/F.N.R.S., Louvain-la-Neuve, Belgium xvii xviii Contributors Selami Varlik Departmentof Philosophy, Istanbul 29 Mayis University,
Istanbul, Turkey
Mohammed Chaouki Zine Departmentof Philosophy, Université de Tlemcen,
Oran, Algeria Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical
Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s
Hermeneutical Approach
Ahmad Bostani

Henry Corbin may be construed as the first scholar who deliberately
tried to put into practice the principles of philosophical hermeneutics in order to
understand and interpret the Islamic philosophical tradition, at least with respect to
the Iranian and Shi’ite context. Nonetheless, he did not merely transfer these principles from Western philosophy onto Islamic culture in order to apply them to anew
context. Instead, he tried to establish aharmonious relationship between the Islamic
exegetical tradition, on the one hand, and some aspects of European phenomenology and hermeneutics, on the other hand. Hence, his approach represents amasterful and consistent combination of Western and Eastern interpretative traditions.
Over the last decades, Corbin’shermeneutics has gradually become amajor
approach within the study of Islamic philosophy and mysticism.1 Corbin’sapproach
has been widely adopted and applied by several scholars in the Islamic world, as
well as by some European philosophers and orientalists. Moreover, his philosophical ideas have profoundly affected contemporary Iranian intellectuals from various
political strands, including traditionalists, anti-modernists, and sometimes
secularists.2 1 Dimitri Gutas, “The Study of Arabic Philosophy in the Twentieth Century: An Essay on the
Historiography of Arabic Philosophy,” British Journal of Middle Eastern Studies 29, no. 1, 16–19.
2
See for instance Ali Mirsepassi, Transnationalism in Iranian Political Thought, The Life and
Times of Ahmad Fardid, 111–144; Matthijs Van den Bos, “Transnational
Orientalism, Henry Corbin in Iran,” Anthropos 100, 113–125. A. Bostani (*)
Departmentof Political Science, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran
e-mail: abostani@khu.ac.ir
© Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2022
S. Camilleri, S. Varlik (eds.), Philosophical Hermeneutics and Islamic Thought,
Contributions to Hermeneutics 10, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-92754-7_6 95

96 A. Bostani My chapter aims to discuss Corbin’shermeneutics, especially from the pointof
view of contemporary philosophical hermeneutics. Iwill strive to demonstrate that
his reading of traditional texts suffers from several methodological flaws rooted in
his metaphysical Weltanschauung, which may be called a “cosmic” worldview.
Thus, Iwill try to critically analyze Corbin’shermeneutics with respect to this cosmic stance. Iwill restrict my critical appraisal only to the hermeneutical aspects of
Corbin’sapproach in order to show that it may lead to amisunderstaning of the
Islamic and Iranian traditional texts. However, one can also critically engage with
other aspects of Corbin'sapproach, e.g, concerning phenomenology or political
theology. 1 Corbin and Philosophical Hermeneutics

Corbin rarely discussed the assumptions upon which his own methodology of interpretation is based. The only exception may be along biographical interview where
he elucidated some salient points about his intellectual project.3 However, one can
recognize the elements of his hermeneutical approach from his writings on the exegetical doctrine of Muslim philosophers. His approach is an amalgam of several
Eastern and European interpretative theories. On the Western side, he was considerably influenced by the philosophical and phenomenological hermeneutics of
Heidegger, on the one hand, and by Christian (mostly Protestant) hermeneutics, on
the other hand.
The influence of Heidegger on Corbin’smethod is profound. He was apupil of
Heidegger for several years and, is well known, was the very translator of some of
his works into French in the 1930s. According to him, as explained in abovementioned interview, Heidegger’sphilosophical hermeneutics was to be conceived as a
key (clavis hermeneutica) in order to unlock all kind of doors, and first and foremost
the hidden or esoteric meaning underlying the exoteric statements of Islamic theology. He added that, “to make use of akey to open alock is not at all the same thing
as to confuse the key with the lock.”4 Therefore, Corbin drew aclear-cut distinction
between Heidegger’sanalytic and his worldview, accepting the former and rejecting
the latter:
It has long since been observed that the “Analytic” – which is the application of the
Heideggerian Hermeneutics – already tacitly posits afundamental philosophical choice, a
conception of the world, a Weltanschauung. This choice announces itself at the horizon
within which the “Analytic” of the Da of the Dasein is deployed. But it is not at all neces- 3 Henry Corbin, “De Heidegger à Sohravardi: Entretien avec Philippe Nemo,” in Henry Corbin:
Cahier de l’Herne, Christian Jambet, ed., 23–37.
4
Ibid, 25.

Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s Hermeneutical Approach 97 sary to adhere to this tacit Weltanschauung to make use of all the resources of an “Analytic”
of this Da-sein.5

Corbin learned from Heidegger at least two crucial elements. First, hermeneutics is
philosophy’scentral concern. In his eyes, Heidegger’sgreat merit thus remains in
his having centered the actof philosophizing in hermeneutics itself. The second
consideration is the priority of ontology over understanding. In other terms, the
mode of understanding is in accordance with, or conditioned by, the mode of being.6
Thus, hermeneutics “does not consist in deliberating upon concepts; it is essentially
the unveiling or revelation of that which is happening within us.”7
Corbin was also attracted by theological hermeneutics of monotheistic Religions.
He criticized Heidegger’sdisciples (and Heidegger himself, to some extent) for
overlooking the theological dimensions of hermeneutics, or what he called “the
phenomenon of the sacred book.”8 Through this concept, he was emphasizing the
common features of interpretative traditions stemming from the Abrahamic, monotheistic religions. The idea of monotheism has acrucial place in Corbin’sconception of hermeneutics, and, as Iwill later demonstrate, it laid the ground for his
ontological and epistemological system.
With regard to Islamic thought, the Shi’ite and philosophical ta’wil that characterizes esoteric Islam fascinated Corbin. Ta’wil means going back from the exoteric
or apparent aspect (ẓāhir) of atext into its esoteric meaning (bāṭin), in order to
unveil its hidden truth.9 Let us notoverlook that this return is meant as the restoration of an original meaning, which had been somehow lost in interpretation along
the way.10 Corbin’sconception of ta’wil is further based upon an ontological or
cosmological system, which comprehends the unity of the Universe in terms of two
arcs of descent and ascent.11 According to this hermeneutical strategy, which also
draws upon Neo-Platonic doctrines, amystical text includes insights into the unfolding of the universe and the relationship of the individual soul to this process.12
Corbin thus considers hermeneutics to be aprocess of unveiling and uncovering.
Corbin’shermeneutics also involves ahierarchy of universes, or spiritual realms,
each of them representing aspecific manifestation of being and being related to a

5
Ibid, 30. Quoted from the English translation: http://www.imagomundi.com.br/espiritualidade/
corbin_heid_suhr.pdf
6
Henry Corbin, A History of Islamic Philosophy, trans. Philipp Sherrard, 1.
7
Henry Corbin, “De Heidegger à Sohravardi,” 25.
8
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. III, 300–312.
9
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. II, 181.
10
Henry Corbin, Avicenna and the Visionary Recital, trans. William R. Trask, 29.
11
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. III, 15.
12
Aaron W. Hughes, The Texture of the Divine, Imagination in Medieval Islamic and Jewish
Thought, 13. 98 A. Bostani

particular level of understanding.13 There are several levels of interpretations (tafsīr,
ta’wīl, and tafhīm), the highestof which is based on divine inspiration.14 There is
also atypology of spirituals and sages, whose places are determined according to
the extentof their participation in the higher worlds. This hierarchy of meanings and
worlds is what was missing in Heidegger and that Corbin eventually found in the
Iranian wisdom.15
With regard to all this, Corbin’shermeneutics can be construed as an approach to
understanding and reading that forms an amalgam of phenomenology, philosophical hermeneutics, theological interpretation, and Eastern exegetical traditions, yet
with adeeply personal twist. Corbin indeed considers his approach as akind of
phenomenology stricto sensu, that is, areturn to the phenomenon itself, echoing the
Islamic esoteric kashf al-mahjūb. Although this approach has some obvious commonalities with twentieth century philosophical hermeneutics (including the priority of ontology in Heidegger, and the authority of tradition in Gadamer), we can spot
some significant differences, the most importantof which being the said cosmic
conception of interpretation. Let us get acloser look at it. In Corbin’sphenomenology, understanding atext requires the participation in ahigher spiritual world or
connecting to acosmic order:
Philosophical thought in Islam moves in two counter yet complementary directions: issuing
from the Origin (mabdā’), and returning (ma‘ād) to the Origin, issue and return both taking
place in avertical dimension. Forms are thoughtof as being in space rather than in time.
Our thinkers perceive the world not as “evolving” in ahorizontal and rectilinear direction,
but as ascending: the past is not behind us but beneath our feet. From this axis stem the
meanings of the divine Revelations, each of these meanings corresponding to aspiritual
hierarchy, to athe level of the universe that issues from the threshold of meta-history.16

Thus, Corbin’shermeneutics presupposes a “vertical” experience or what in mystic
terminology has been called epiphany.17 To take only one representative example,
this verticality is sharply opposed to Gadamer’sconception of hermeneutics, where
interpretation as historical consciousness occurs through an interaction or fusion of
horizons following ahorizontal axis.18 In sum, Corbin’shermeneutical approach
requires the acceptance of higher worlds and cosmic truths, as well as aconnection
to ahigher reality. Interpretation is acosmic process based on apresential 13 Henry Corbin, Swedenborg and Esoteric Islam, trans. Leonard Fox, 38.
14
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. III, 215.
15
In the interview mentioned above he tells about “the decisive moment in which Iwas drawn
towards hermeneutical levels that had not been foreseen by the Heideggerian Analytic that Ihad at
my disposal.” Henry Corbin, “De Heidegger à Sohravardî”, 32.
16
Henry Corbin, A History of Islamic Philosophy, 14.
17
Anthony J. Steinbock, Phenomenology and Mysticism, the Verticality of Religious Experience, 15.
18
Hans-Georg Gadamer, Truth and Method, trans. Joel Weisheimer and Donald G. Marshall, 302. Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s Hermeneutical Approach 99 knowledge (al-‘ilm al-ḥuḍuri) wherein prevails ahierarchy of meanings and levels
of interpreters. Let us now address afew pitfalls. 2 An Uncritical Hermeneutics

Corbin’shermeneutics lacks any critical dimension. Criticism presupposes adistance and, as briefly shown, his reading method, to agreat extent, is extracted from
the texts and doctrines he studied without further consideration.19 According to him,
the ideal interpreter should indeed be at the same ontological and epistemological
level as the text and its author. Since ta’wil requires presence, there can be no distance, no discrepancy between the reader and the author when the actof reading is
taking place.
In this approach, there is no second order analytic, or what Pierre Aubenque has
called “external position.”20 Due to the superiority that ahistorian of philosophy
enjoys over his subjectof study, he may find in aphilosophical text some meanings
that are unfamiliar even to the author himself. Aubenque particularly mentions the
way Heidegger interpreted the central themes of Kant’s Critique of Pure Reason. In
his Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics, Heidegger states:
In order to wring from what the words say, what it is they want to say, every interpretation
must use violence. Such violence cannot be roving arbitrariness. The power of an idea
which shines forth must drive and guide the laying-out. Only in the power of this idea can
an interpretation risk what is always audacious, namely, entrusting itself to the concealed
inner passion of awork in order to be able, through his, to place itself within the unsaid and
force it into speech.21

In this sense, Corbin’shermeneutics proves to be too soft, because to close to its
objects, here, the Islamic tradition. Yet such astate of affairs relies on apeculiar
rationale. Corbin explains this by drawing adistinction between the attitudes of
historians and phenomenologists, respectively, towards the past. The historian
establishes the course of events of the past without feeling him or herself responsible, but the phenomenologist undertakes aheavy responsibility for the past in that
“he makes himself answerable for its future”.22 In aletter to Ivanow, Corbin writes
about his approach to the Ismaili texts: 19 As John Walbridge explained, Corbin sees no difference between saying, “Suhrawardisays the
history of philosophy is thus” and saying, “the history of philosophy is thus.” John Walbridge, The
Wisdom of the Mystic East. Suhrawardi and Platonic Orientalism, 109.
20
Pierre Aubenque, “Le conflit actuel des interprétations: analytique ou herméneutique?,” in
Comment écrire l’histoire de la philosophie?, Yves Charles Zarka, ed., 50.
21
Martin Heidegger, Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics, trans. Richard Taft, 141.
22
Henry Corbin, “De Heidegger a Sohravardi”, 27. 100 A. Bostani pour moi, le philosophe doit prendre en charge le stock d’idées de son auteur et le porter à
son maximum de signification. C’est l’Ismaélisme dans son ensemble que j’avais en vue et
j’en ai commenté et amplifie les philosophèmes. Comme si j’étais moi-même Ismaélien.
Celan’est possible que pour une sympathie congénitale. Faute de cette sympathie, le philosophe égaré risque au contraire de porter l’auteur ou son école au maximum de platitude.23

According to Corbin, the phenomenological approach involves asympathetic participation in order tofill the gap between the author and the reader. Such agap, in
philosophical hermeneutics, is notonly unbridgeable, but also is the condition of
interpretation. For instance, as Ricœur maintains, the text produces adouble eclipse
of the reader and the writer, because the reader is absent from the actof writing and
the writer is absent from the actof reading.24
On the other hand, the epoche, i.e., the bracketing of religious faith, plays acrucial role in the phenomenology of religion. As Ricœur notes,
Is it possible for aphenomenology of the sacred to stay within the limits of neutral attitude
governed by the epoche, by the bracketing of absolute reality and of every question concerning the absolute? The epoche requires that Iparticipate in the belief in the reality of the
religious object, but in aneutralized mode; that Ibelieve with the believer, but without
positing absolutely the objectof his belief.25

Phenomenology begins when “we interrupt lived experience in order to signify it,”26
since it is through the epoche that meaning can appear as meaning. Although Corbin
presented himself as aphenomenologistof Sprit, this epoche plays little or no role
in his undertakings. Conversely, he felt it necessary for aphenomenologist to put
himself in the place of those whom he studied and to somehow repeator take over
their experience. Then, this attitude is based on the sympathetic participation carried
unto the pointof identification. That is why, as Charles Adams argued, he may not
be considered aphenomenologistof religion, but rather a “missionary of
spirituality.”27
In Corbin’sview, the religious fact takes place only in the soul of the believer, not
in the course of historical events.28 Accordingly, notonly the historicity of religious
and spiritual doctrines, but also some basic principles of phenomenology are simply
ignored.29 One of the most important breakthroughs of Husserlian phenomenology
for philosophical hermeneutics is the idea of “inter-subjectivity”, declined in the 23 Henry Corbin & Wladimir Ivanow, Correspondance Corbin-Ivanow, Sabine Schmidtke, ed., 126.
24
Paul Ricœur, Du texte à l’action, 155.
25
Paul Ricœur, De l’interprétation, Essais sur Freud, 39. Quoted
from the English translation: Paul Ricœur, Freud and Philosophy, trans. Denis Savage, 29.
26
Paul Ricœur, Du texte à l’action, 64.
27
Charles Adams, “The Hermeneutics of Henry Corbin”, in Approaches to Study of Islam, Richard
Martin, ed., 142.
28
Henry Corbin, En islam iranien, t. I,, xix.
29
Hamid Algar, “The Study of Islam: The Work of Henry Corbin,” Religious Studies 6, no. 2, 90. Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s Hermeneutical Approach 101

form of “lifeworld” in Gadamer30 and the “world of the text” in Ricœur. Corbin is
completely missing this intersubjective dimension: his cosmic-ontological hermeneutics indeed seems to operate on the subjective level only. When alleging that the
religious phenomenatake place in the soul of the believer and that the phenomenologist should recover them at the same level, he thus remains stuck in the subject/
object dichotomy and fails to overcome the serious philosophical shortcomings of
such an idea.
The imaginal world or mundus imaginalis performs apivotal role in Corbin’s
doctrine of interpretation. Abrief comparison between Corbin’s “imaginal world”
and Ricœur’s “world of the text” may be useful in order to understand the difference
between psycho-cosmological31 and intersubjective hermeneutics. For Ricœur, what
must be interpreted in atext is aproposed world that the interpreter may inhabit and
wherein he may projectone of his ownmost possibilities. This proposed world “is
not so much adifferent world, as aworld expanded to encompass its possibilities.
In other words, we are directed at the same world; we just come to see it differently.”32
Nonetheless, Corbin’simaginal world, which serves as the ontological reference for
his hermeneutics, is an autonomous world: in the hierarchy of the worlds, it is
located above the material world, not in parallel with it. This realm may not be
reduced to any historical community or intersubjective sphere. In philosophical
hermeneutics, the relationship between the reader and the text is horizontal, although
in Corbin’shermeneutics this link is vertical. This is the reason why the dialectic of
distanciation and belonging (appropriation), as well as the fusion of horizons, has
no place in Corbin’sapproach.
The dearth of critical judgment in Corbin’shermeneutics is deeply rooted in the
very definition of the key conceptof the “text”, especially with respect to the Islamic
context. In his well-known article “What is atext?”, Ricœur defines the text as “any
discourse fixed by writing.” In this context, interpretation would be an attempt to
understand the meaning of the text through this distance installed by fixation. This
distance is between the condition of utterance and the intentions of the author.
According to Ricœur, this distanciation adds acritical momentum to the process of
interpretation. Thus, the effectof distanciation is that the text can be considered
independently and on its own terms. Corbin’shermeneutics cannot accept this distanciaion, since to him atradition may not be fixed. He construes tradition as aliving and dynamic world in which we can participate through the creative (ontological)
imagination. This world is the place where we can meet prophets, sages, Imams,
mystics, and philosophers. The higher your cosmic level gets, the more your interpretation is complete. For instance, S. H. Nasr, an influential disciple of Corbin,
insisting on the importance of oral transmission in Iranian mysticism, declared that
30

Hans-Georg Gadamer, Truth and Method, 239–242.
Henry Corbin, Creative Imagination in the Sufism of Ibn ‘Arabi, trans. Ralph Manheim, 254.
32
Leslie MacAvory, “Distanciation and Epoché: the Influence of Husserl on Ricoeur’s
Hermeneutics”, in Hermeneutics and Phenomenology in Ricœur, Scott Davidson & Marc-Antoine
Vallée, eds., 25.
31 102 A. Bostani some masters of theoretical mysticism “have claimed direct contact with Ibn Arabi
in the invisible world (‘ālam al-ghayb) besides being heirs to the historical oral
tradition of his school.”33 It is obvious that such aview of tradition, which even
encompasses visionary encounters, has nothing to do with fixation, and is not fit to
keep tradition at adistance in order to criticize it.
Another non-critical aspectof Corbin’shermeneutics relates to his understanding of symbol. The symbolic dimension, as amedian realm between the material
world and the human mind, performs aprimary role. The imaginal world, in many
respects, is the world of symbols or the symbolic expression of cosmic truths.
Corbin also interprets the intellectual doctrines of Iranian philosophers (e.g.,
Avicenna and Suhrawardī) according to their recital treatises rather than their systematic philosophical works. Corbin draws aclear-cut distinction between symbol
and allegory:
L’allégorie se maintient sur le même plan d’être et d’évidence que celui de la perception
commune. L’exégèse allégorique exténue et dénature les évènements visionnaires, parce
qu’en fait elle est incapable de comprendre la réalité d’évènements autres que les évènements sensibles.34

According to Corbin, symbols reveal something that cannot be expressed otherwise.
It is the only possible expression of the thing symbolized, while allegory is artificial
figuration of generalities or abstractions that can perfectly be expressed and known
by other ways.35
Corbin insists that higher truths are ineffable and only symbol can approach
them. Symbol is like silence: it says and it does not say but in the end it cannot be
deciphered once and for all.36 In Corbin’shermeneutics, symbols are irrefutable:
“one can understand them, one can refuse them, but they are not such thatone can
refute them.”37 This take probably stems from Suhrawradī’sintroduction to his treatise on The Philosophy of Illumination (ḥikmat al-ishrāq) where he maintains “there
is no refutation of the symbol (ramz).”38 In such aconfiguration, criticism is impossible since the structure of the text, as anetwork of symbols, is in away that exceeds 33 Seyyed Hossein Nasr, “Oral Transmission and the Book in Islamic Education: the Spoken and
the Written Word,” Journal of Islamic Studies 3, no. 1, 8–9.
34
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. IV, 86–87.
35
The distinction drawn by Corbin between symbol and allegory is highly controversial among
Islamic scholars. For instance, Gutas describes it as “highly subjective” and “quite inaccurate and
misleading”. See Dimitri Gutas, Avicenna and the Aristotelian Tradition, 337. For the hermeneutic significance of the distinction allegory/symbol, see William
Franke, “Symbol and Allegory,” in The Routledge Companion to Hermeneutics, Jeff Malpas &
Hans-Helmuth Gander, eds., 367–377.
36
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. IV, 72.
37
Henry Corbin, “De Heidegger a Sohravardi”, 28.
38
Œuvres philosophique et mystiques de Suhrawardi, Henry Corbin, ed., 10 (Arabic text).

Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s Hermeneutical Approach 103 any kind of evaluation.39 The important point here is that Corbin does not confine
this irrefutability to the sacred texts such as the Qur’an; instead, he applies the same
hermeneutic strategy towards non-religious and even non-spiritual texts. For
instance, Ferdowsi’s Shâh-Nâmeh, aliterary national epic, is interpreted by him in
the exact same way.40
Corbin’sattitude seems to refer to the ancient doctrine according in which symbol is not considered alinguistic phenomenon. Yet this idea may lead to akind of
impossibility of philosophical hermeneutics, since the latter precisely associates
symbol with language through interpretation. As Paul Ricœur rightly argued:
The problem of symbolism has turned out to be coextensive with the problem of language
itself. There is no symbolism prior to man who speaks, even though the power of symbols
is rooted more deeply, in the expressiveness of the cosmos, in what desire wants to say, in
the varied image-contents that men have.41 Thus, every symbol, because it is ahuman linguistic phenomenon, may be subject
to criticism. Corbin’sdismissal of the “linguistic turn” of contemporary philosophy—which has exerted acrucial influence on contemporary hermeneutics—is
another weakness of Corbin’shermeneutical approach. 3 Interpretation or Over-Interpretation?

Paul Ricœur distinguishes between two antithetical approaches to interpretation,
leading to two kinds of hermeneutics characteristic of the modern era. The firstone,
interpretation as a “recollection of meaning,” treats the symbol or text as awindow
onto asacred reality and attempts to recover the meaning hidden in it. The other
one, interpretation as an “exercise of suspicion”, treats the same symbol or text as a
false reality that must be shattered.42 Phenomenology of religion (in the works of
Van der Leeuw, Leenhardt, and Eliade) and demythologizing hermeneutics (mainly
in Bultmann) are examples of the first kind of interpretation. At first glance, Corbin’s
hermeneutics also may be considered as an instance of the recollection of meaning,
yet it is not. It is true that this sortof hermeneutics is based on faith, but, as Ricœur
points out, this is no longer the first faith of the simple soul, but rather the second
faith of someone who has engaged in hermeneutics, that is a “post-critical” faith.43
Conversely, Corbin’sapproach is based on apre-critical interpretation of religious
39 Compare with what Ernst Cassirer has written about myth: “Amyth is in asense invulnerable. It
is impervious to rational arguments; it cannot be refuted by syllogism.” See Ernst Cassirer, The
Myth of the State, 296.
40
Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. II, 212; Pierre Lory, “Henry Corbin: His Work and Influence,”
in History of Islamic Philosophy, Seyyed Hossein Nasr & Oliver Leaman, eds., 2047.
41
Paul Ricœur, De l’interprétation, 26; Paul Ricœur, Freud and Philosophy, 16.
42
Paul Ricœur, De l’interprétation, 38–46.
43
Ibid, 38. 104 A. Bostani

and spiritual texts. Therefore, Corbin’shermeneutics may be seen as aparamount
instance of over-interpretation.
Freud coined the term “over-interpretation” in the contextof his theory of dream
interpretation.44 Yet Iuse the term in accordance with Umberto Eco, who thereby
means the attempt to read and re-read atext in order tofind ahidden message or
secret beneath the words. There is an infinite chain of interpretations in which the
meaning is not accessible to everyone, but to alimited circle of chosen people.
As Eco explains, the Hermetic and Gnostic tradition of interpretation is an exemplary case of over-interpretation. This approach has been called by Eco “Hermetic
semiosis”, for the text is seen as an open-ended universe and language is deemed
unable to grasp aunique and pre-existing meaning. There is a “syndrome of the
secret” in this tradition: “truth is secret and any questioning of the symbols and
enigmas will never reveal ultimate truth but simply displace the secret elsewhere.”45
Based on adeep suspicion toward apparent meaning, the chain of interpretations
leads this approach to exceeding the bounds of legitimate interpretation. Eco
explains that this hermetic approach is not restricted to sacred or religious texts but
can be applied to awider range of texts named “sacred secular” texts.46
Corbin’sapproach to Shi’ite and mystical texts serves as apeculiar example of
over-interpretation. He assumes that theses texts cover several layers of meaning
and several levels of interpretation.47 Accordingly, interpreting them is unveiling and uncovering these layers and levels. Yet the task is infinite, for there is no
ultimate stage where one could claim to have discovered the very secretof the text,
that is, to have exhausted its resources. If such is the case, it is because the hierarchy
of meanings is related to the cosmic hierarchy, on the one hand, and because the text
represents the whole universe, on the other hand. Thus, each text is amicrocosm and
the interpreter, according to his cosmic level, may have access to adeeper meaning.
We ought to say that in Corbin’sview the actof interpretation occurs ontologically
within ahermeneutic triangle: the soul (of the interpreter), the text, and the Universe
or Cosmos. This triangle draws upon an ancient “theory of correspondence”48
according to which there is an extensive correspondence between the text, the
human soul and the cosmological structure of the world.49
Now let us go back to Corbin’semphasis on the significance of symbol and insist
on how it reflects his disdain toward language. In his apophatic theology, the
supreme truth is unknowable, unnamable, ineffable, and can be expressed only by
negation and approximation.50 But this idea of ineffability is not compatible with

45
Umberto Eco, Interpretation and Over-interpretation, 35.

Henry Corbin, En Islam iranien, t. I, 61–63.

Henry Corbin, Swedenborg and Esoteric Islam, 40.

Henry Corbin, En Islam Iranien, t. III, 324.

Henry Corbin, A History of Islamic Philosophy, 79. Cosmic Hermeneutics: A Critical Appraisal of Henry Corbin’s Hermeneutical Approach 105

hermeneutical requirements. It is true that, as some phenomenologists have shown,
language has several limitations and one can perfectly think of other non-linguistic
systems of representation.51 But unlike them, Corbin’scosmic hermeneutics holds a
written (spiritual) text as the higher world in miniature, as amicrocosm. As mentioned above, Umberto Eco accurately put forth that Hermetic semiosis encounters
aparadox: it transforms the whole universe into atext (which is amere linguistic
phenomenon), and at the same time, it empties language of any communicative
power.52 This very flaw affects Corbin’shermeneutics deeply.
In Corbin’swritings, over-interpretation is coupled with another important limiting feature, which is selectiveness. His approach is indeed highly selective. He
picks some specific elements of atext (or some specific texts from an entire corpus)
and tends to read too much into them. This is what Aaron Hughes has described as
Corbin’s “maximum reading”, i.e., reading selectively and exceeding the boundaries of interpretation.53 As Eco maintains, the emphasis on similarity is one of the
main features of Hermetic approach to texts, since it paves the way for connecting
everything witheverything else.54 That explains why Corbin’shermeneutics insists
on the resemblances, rather than differences, between doctrines and ideas. For
instance, the term “theosophy” is used by him to describe awide range of sciences
including Kalām, philosophy, theoretical mysticism, and mythology. He thus disregards their differences with respect to end, method and subject, and closes his eyes
to substantial disagreements or even historical conflicts among philosophers, theologians, and mystics.
The scholars of Islamic studies have indicated several instances of overinterpretation in Corbin’sworks. His conception of Avicenna’s Eastern Philosophy,55
Suhrawardī’ssagesse orientale,56 Mullā Ṣadrā’stheosophy,57 and Ibn ʿArabī’stheory of ta’wīl58 are some prominent examples of over-interpretations that have been
criticized by Islamic scholars. To this we can add other examples of misconception
such as: over-reading Sunni doctrines in order tofind Shi’ite dimension in them,
regarding Sufism as aversion of Shi’ism, considering the Imaginal world, which is
supposed to be the median world between intelligible and sensible worlds, as stemming from an ancient Iranian doctrine, and finally overlooking rational, historical,
juridical, and exoteric aspects of Islamic (and particularly Shi’ite) beliefs.59 One can

termed “permanent hermeneutics”, in the works of some of some of his disciples
such as Christian Jambet,60 Gilbert Durand,61 Daryush Shayegan,62 and MohammadAli Amir-Moezzi.63 4 Conclusion

Henry Corbin’shermeneutical approach is based upon aspecific metaphysical system with ontological and epistemological prejudices. These prejudices, which have
been put forth in this paper as resulting from the cosmic worldview, inscribe everything in relation to some sortof cosmic dynamic. The world is avertical hierarchy
that mirrors an emanation process, with arcs of ascent and descent. In this view,
everything in the sublunary or material world has an archetype or form in the imaginal world. Everything has two aspects: esoteric and exoteric. The apparent should
be understood in the lightof the hidden.
Thus, we can say that Corbin’sphilosophical activity is “hermeneutical” through
and through since it includes apermanent movement from the exoteric to the esoteric in order to discover or recover the hidden cosmic secret. For him, hermeneutics
is unveiling, and traditional texts are windows open to the higher levels of being.
Although Husserl and Heidegger have influenced Corbin in some respects, the technical concepts of the twentieth century hermeneutics and phenomenology (such as
horizontality, historicity, dialogue, phenomenological reduction, boundaries of
legitimate interpretation, conflictof interpretations, critical distanciation, and so on)
find no place in his system. 60 Christian Jambet, La logique des orientaux.
